dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a suspected cardiac arrest at Apt 10, Richmond.
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
He was unconscious, and a lady was doing chest compressions with sputum coming from his mouth.
So he's not breathing normally and without a pulse? Was there anything else you noticed?
GCS of 3-no eye opening, verbal, or motor response to stimuli.
Pupils are pinpoint and fixed-no signs of eye opening observed initially, but reactive after naloxone administration.
No motor response to external stimuli initially, but some movement noted after reversal.
Airway is not patent enough-sputum is present, so we need to secure it with an airway adjunct.
Agonal respirations present-very abnormal breathing pattern before any improvement with BVM.
Respiration rate is 14 per minute-labored and irregular during initial evaluation, which improved with O2.
Strong peripheral pulses-capnography was initially elevated and improved after medications.
Blood pressure is 161 over 93 mmHg-elevated but remains in acceptable range during treatment.
Heart rate is 101 beats per minute-tachycardia noted, increased to 121 post-naloxone and then stabilized during transport.
O2 saturation is 95%-initially as low as 20-25%, improved to 90-94% with O2 administration.
Blood glucose is 327 mg/dL-no further measurements were taken due to protocol restrictions.
Sinus tachycardia on EKG-consistent with opioid and stimulant toxicity during reversal period.
Potential overdose based on his pupillary response. First dose of naloxone is being administered now-0.5 mg IV per protocol for overdose reversal.
IV access established-18G in right AC to administer naloxone. Aseptic technique used with saline lock applied.
Administering 0.5 mg naloxone-monitor for improvement. Subsequent 0.25 mg doses added at five-minute intervals following response drop.
I found him unconscious after a Halloween party. He said he wanted to "authenticate" his drug dealer costume.
A woman started doing chest compressions immediately after finding him unresponsive.
You felt "strange" before passing out, right?
Yeah, it hit me fast. The next thing I knew I woke up with EMS around me.
Your name and age, please?
I'm James, 21 years old. I never overdose on drugs and don't use them regularly.
What medications do you take, James?
I take Lexapro for my anxiety. No history of drug issues.
You're diaphoretic, cyanotic, and pale-clear signs of poor oxygenation.
Pupils remain pinpoint and fixed-suggesting opioid toxicity.
Inserting a 28-French Nasopharyngeal Airway to assist with oxygenation-EKG used to continuously monitor during treatment.
Administering naloxone now-watch for any clinical improvement as per protocol.
Attaching 4-lead ECG monitor-part of our protocol for overdose-initial rhythm noted as sinus tachycardia.
Pulse increased to 121 per minute-respirations now at 27 per minute after the first dose.
Blood pressure is 130 over 80-stable for now, will continue monitoring.
SpO2 decreased to 91-not great, but capnography is improving after oxygen use.
Starting BVM at 15 LPM with O2-NPA in place to aid breathing.
Another 0.25 mg of naloxone given-awaiting signs of awakening per protocol.
Pulse now at 111, SpO2 improved to 94%. Blood pressure is 123 over 86.
Patient is alert and oriented to time, person, place-GCS of 15 noted.
Resp rate at 22-still elevated but looking better.
Giving another 0.25 mg of naloxone-continue per overdose protocol.
Respirations dropped to 19-no labored breathing noted.
Any pain in your head, chest, or abdomen?
No pain reported anywhere-just disoriented but okay.
Initiating log roll onto stretcher-no signs of trauma during repositioning.
Transport destination is VCU Medical Center for further observation-ED has been pre-notified of patient's risk.
Patient secured in semi Fowler's-straps locked and ready for transport.
Confirming no movement issues with limbs-active motor functions observed during transport.
Patient reports no pain-moves limbs appropriately when prompted.
Vital signs stable on transport-final SpO2 at 92% with controlled respiration.
History of anxiety and use of Lexapro reported-no drug allergy noted in records.
Cocaine ingestion reported before incident-no prior overdose or drug use history mentioned.
IV in right AC with saline lock-no complications reported during transport.
Oxygen administered via NC at 4-6 LPM-SpO2 levels between 91-92% at handoff.
No significant trauma noted-visual exam from arrival was limited to posture and pallor.
Vital tracking followed protocol-each 15 minutes or after medication push with recorded intervals.
NPA is still in place-respiratory support maintained without obstruction noted.
EKG trend consistent with opioid reversal-no acute infarction identified.
GCS of 15-fully orientated now after initial unresponsiveness.
James remains disoriented but cooperative-can perform joint movements when prompted.
Blood glucose remains at 327-documented per protocol at initial assessment.
Call reported as Cardiac Arrest/Death-patient treated and stabilized prior to transport.
Ensure ED staff logs all interventions and vital signs-essential during transition.
Vital deviations and interventions recorded-handoff data is complete according to protocol.
Transport with Reeves stretcher and RFD-uninterrupted process recorded.
Patient arrived to VCU ED-notification provided for ED preparations.
NPA remains in place-ED staff alerted prior to arrival.
